title_en: Introduction to the Divine Names and the Methodology of Affirming Attributes
title_ar: مقدمة في الأسماء الحسنى ومنهج إثبات الصفات
content_en:
Imam Abu Bakr Ahmad ibn al-Husayn al-Bayhaqi (384–458 AH / 994–1066 CE) composed Al-Asma was-Sifat as a systematic treatment of Allah's beautiful names and sublime attributes, drawing on the Quran, the authenticated Sunnah, and the positions of the early Muslim scholars. Al-Bayhaqi was one of the greatest hadith scholars of his era, a devoted follower of the Ash'ari theological school, and a leading figure in the Shafi'i legal tradition. His approach in this work reflects the balanced methodology of the Ash'ari school: affirming the attributes of Allah as they are mentioned in the texts while negating any resemblance between Allah and His creation.

The theological question at the heart of this work is how the Muslim should understand the divine attributes mentioned in the Quran and hadith — attributes such as Allah's hand, His face, His settling over the Throne (istiwa), His descending to the lower heaven, and countless others. The early Muslim community understood these texts to affirm real attributes of Allah while firmly denying that these attributes resemble anything in creation, following the Quranic verse: 'There is nothing like unto Him, and He is the All-Hearing, All-Seeing' (42:11). This affirmation without likening (ithbat bila tashbih) became the foundational principle of orthodox Sunni theology.

Al-Bayhaqi begins by establishing the obligatory nature of knowing Allah through His names and attributes. Knowledge of Allah is the foundation of all religious life — prayer, trust, love, fear, hope, and all acts of worship presuppose some understanding of who Allah is and what He is like. The Quran repeatedly invites human beings to reflect on Allah's attributes as a path to deeper faith and more sincere worship. The Prophet (peace be upon him) said: 'Allah has ninety-nine names — one hundred minus one — whoever memorizes them will enter Paradise.' This hadith indicates that learning and understanding the divine names is an act of worship in itself.

The methodology that al-Bayhaqi follows in this work is rooted in three principles. First, every attribute mentioned in the Quran or in an authenticated hadith must be affirmed without denial or distortion of the text. Second, every similarity between Allah and His creation must be negated, since Allah is unique and incomparable. Third, when a text mentions an attribute whose precise meaning or mode of existence is unclear, the scholar has two legitimate options: either to interpret the attribute in a manner consistent with Allah's transcendence (ta'wil), or to affirm the attribute while committing its meaning to Allah's knowledge (tafwid al-ma'na), saying: 'Allah has an attribute He calls by this name; its reality is known only to Him, and we affirm it without asking how.'

The Ash'ari school, of which al-Bayhaqi is a distinguished representative, is particularly associated with the first approach — ta'wil — when the literal meaning of an attribute text would imply bodily form or resemblance to creation. However, al-Bayhaqi and the Ash'ari scholars are careful to note that ta'wil is not a flight from the text but a means of honoring it: by interpreting 'hand' as 'power' or 'grace,' or 'settling' (istiwa) as 'sovereignty and domination' (qahr wal-istila), they preserve the affirmation of a real divine attribute while removing the erroneous implication of physical form. This approach is supported by the Quran's own use of figurative language and by the practice of many Companions and early scholars.

Al-Bayhaqi also addresses the question of the divine names (al-asma al-husna). He affirms that the ninety-nine names mentioned in hadith are not an exhaustive list but a highlighting of the most well-known; Allah has names known only to Himself, as mentioned in the hadith of the intercession. The divine names are all 'husna' — beautiful and perfect — because they indicate attributes of absolute perfection free from any deficiency. Names implying limitation, deficiency, or resemblance to creation cannot be applied to Allah. Each divine name implies an attribute: 'al-Rahman' implies the attribute of mercy; 'al-Hakim' implies wisdom; 'al-Qadir' implies power; 'al-'Alim' implies knowledge — and the attribute implied by each name is real, not merely figurative.

The opening chapter sets the tone for the entire work: reverence for the revealed texts, careful attention to the tradition of the early scholars, theological precision in distinguishing Allah from His creation, and a pastoral concern for helping ordinary Muslims understand their Creator in a way that deepens their faith and purifies their worship. Al-Bayhaqi's al-Asma was-Sifat remains one of the most important works in the Ash'ari theological tradition and a primary reference for scholars studying the orthodox Sunni understanding of the divine attributes.
content_ar: الله تعالى له الأسماء الحسنى والصفات العلى، وإثباتها يكون على وجه يليق بجلاله من غير تشبيه ولا تعطيل.
